name: Shopify Admin API
what_it_does: Shopify's Admin API provides full programmatic access to a Shopify store — managing products, orders, customers, inventory, discounts, and fulfillment. Available as both REST and GraphQL, it powers headless commerce builds and automated store management.
use_cases: Automating product catalog management (create, update, publish listings)
Inventory sync across warehouses and channels
Discount and promotion creation and lifecycle management
Headless storefront builds with Storefront API
not_for: Non-Shopify storefronts (API is tightly coupled to Shopify platform)
High-frequency trading of inventory data (rate limits constrain real-time sync)
Free storefront hosting (requires paid Shopify plan)
best_when: An agent needs to manage a Shopify-based store — processing orders, syncing inventory, or automating product launches.
avoid_when: The merchant is on WooCommerce, Magento, or another non-Shopify platform, or when sub-second inventory updates are required.

auth.notes: Public apps use OAuth2 with merchant-granted scopes. Custom apps use Admin API access tokens generated in the Partner Dashboard. Scopes are granular per resource (read_products, write_orders, etc.). OAuth flow required for multi-merchant apps.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Development stores are free but cannot process real payments
pricing.paid_tiers: Basic $29/mo; Shopify $79/mo; Advanced $299/mo; Plus $2,300/mo. API access included on all paid plans.
pricing.notes: Merchants pay the Shopify subscription; apps built on the API may charge merchants separately via Shopify's billing API. API rate limits scale with plan tier on Plus.

performance.rate_limits: REST: 2 requests/second (leaky bucket, burst 40); GraphQL: cost-based (1000 points/second, queries cost 1-1000 points). Plus plans get 4x higher limits.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: REST errors include HTTP status, message string, and errors array. GraphQL errors surface in the errors field alongside partial data. GraphQL userErrors distinguish user-fixable issues from system errors. Rate limit errors are clear with retry-after headers.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: No explicit idempotency key header on REST. Agents must deduplicate via external state or check for existing resources before creating. GraphQL mutations have no idempotency mechanism.
known_agent_gotchas: GraphQL and REST have different rate limit models — agents mixing both must track separately
API version must be pinned (e.g., 2024-01) and actively migrated; versions sunset after ~1 year
Webhook payloads must be HMAC-verified; missing this is a common security gap
GraphQL mutations return userErrors in the response body — agents must check body, not just HTTP status
Product variants vs products is a common confusion — product creation requires at least one variant
Fulfillment workflows changed significantly in 2023 API versions; old FulfillmentService pattern deprecated
Metafields require knowing namespace/key schema ahead of time

score_rationale: Shopify Admin API is mature and well-documented with strong GraphQL support, but agents face friction from the lack of idempotency keys, short-lived API version windows requiring active migration, and the non-obvious distinction between REST and GraphQL rate limits. The scoped OAuth model is good for security. GraphQL userErrors pattern requires agents to inspect response bodies rather than relying on HTTP status codes alone.
